posts.author: sambandham82
posts.text: The asset quality worsened between 2012-2018 due to massive boom in infra, power, steel,etc., in 2004-2007 mainly. The corporate balance sheet also had huge debt during that period. But now corporate balance sheet is very healthy. So if we are considering overall banking, it requires a credit upmove to happen before asset quality starts detoriating. I think its a long way from here.

posts.author: espl
posts.text: Absolutely agree. The Banks, NBFC’s which are growing right now are growing by lower the asset quality I believe. Margin of error is low, the yields are also not worth the risk. Capital preservation should be key. (So LICHFL not growing should not be taken as a complete negative, need to wait for the negative global cycle to reverse first).

posts.author: gsapte
posts.text: I also feel the same. After RBI reduced repo rate from 6.25%-6.5% to 5.25% in short period, I felt that it was more a mechanical exercise rather than any deep thought process. Nothing has changed after that. FMCG is struggling to grow at 5%, Few banks are growing at 10%, Infrastructure companies are growing at sub par levels except few GOI orders here and there, Auto is growing at sub par levels. I have not seen such low growth in many sectors before 2015. It seems that, not only LIC HFL but many companies are going through fatigue, probably not doing any thing out-of-the-box and hence growth…

posts.author: espl
posts.text: The problem for Banks, NBFCs in general is they are at the peak of their asset quality numbers. Most of them cannot have lower NPA’s then what they currently have right now historically speaking. So, forget about growth but once the asset quality starts deteriorating, valuation which seem attractive right now would start feeling not so cheap. That is my understanding.

posts.author: Prabhat_Mohanty
posts.text: There are no messages in this thread for more than 18 months. This. is understandable considering the fact that no operational improvement is seen. No growth for more than 2 years. The parent company has no road map for growth. And leadership churn is the main reason. Really very sad state of affairs. Considering its brand image and public sector lineage, a lot could have happened. If professional management is brought or strategic disinvestment can ignite the company. If nothing is happening, why management is not making payouts minimum 30% of earning? Company is adding almost 100 rupees to i…
